summary: Help‑Slip‑Franklin trio shines; Let It Grow and Esau standout
blurb: The second set delivers a tight Help On The Way > Slipknot > Franklin's Tower sequence and a soaring Let It Grow with Phil’s bass front‑and‑center. First‑set material feels uneven, but Brent’s keyboards lift the performance and My Brother Esau makes its debut.
review: On March 25, 1983 at Compton Terrace Amphitheatre the Dead offered an uneven first set that stumbled through classics like Cold Rain And Snow and New Minglewood Blues before finding their footing in the second half. The highlight is unquestionably the Help On The Way > Slipknot > Franklin's Tower suite, which several listeners describe as “brilliant” and a showcase for tight interplay between Jerry’s solid guitar work and Bob’s responsive rhythm. Let It Grow follows with Phil Lesh taking the spotlight—his upper‑register runs and melodic bass lines were repeatedly praised. Brent Mydland’s keyboards provide a bright undercurrent throughout, especially on the debut of My Brother Esau, adding a fresh texture to an otherwise mixed performance. The show closes on a high note with Sugar Magnolia and a gentle encore of Don't Ease Me In, leaving fans with a memorable, if uneven, snapshot of the 1983 tour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are explicitly positive, praising the Help‑Slip‑Franklin sequence and Brent's keyboards; three are mixed, noting both strong moments (Let It Grow, Esau debut) and first‑set sloppiness. The overall tone leans modestly positive, warranting a moderate +0.9 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Excellent in‑your‑face Nakamichi cassette capture with crystal‑clear ambience and no reported dropouts, delivering the most engaging listening experience.

key_highlights: Help On The Way > Slipknot > Franklin's Tower sequence stands out as a tight, high‑energy suite.
Let It Grow showcases Phil Lesh’s standout bass work and solid group chemistry.
My Brother Esau makes its debut, highlighted by Brent’s vibrant keyboard textures.
Sugar Magnolia closes the second set on an uplifting note.

band_performance.Jerry: Solid guitar work throughout, though his vocals sounded strained at times.
band_performance.Phil: Bass shines, especially on Let It Grow with melodic upper‑register runs.
band_performance.Bob: Feeds off Jerry’s lead; the two lock in early and drive the second‑set flow.
band_performance.Brent: Keyboard playing is praised as strong and really great, adding color to the set.
